title: The Complete Stable Diffusion Pipeline
concept: Stable Diffusion combines a text encoder, a latent diffusion U-Net, and a VAE decoder into one complete text-to-image generation pipeline.

exercises.prompt: When you type a text prompt into an AI image generator, what do you think happens first?

options.text: The computer understands the words and turns them into meaning it can use
The computer instantly paints pixels with no understanding
The computer searches the internet for a matching photo

exercises.explain: Before any image can be made, the AI first needs to understand your words! That's the very first stop on the pipeline.

cards.title: One Prompt, Three Machines
cards.body: You type 'a dragon reading a book' and — like magic — a picture appears. But it's not one machine doing this. It's actually THREE clever machines working together in a row, like an assembly line. Let's peek behind the curtain!

cards.title: The Full Pipeline
cards.body:
Stable Diffusion is a team of three parts working together:
1) A TEXT ENCODER reads your words and turns them into a meaning-map.
2) A LATENT DIFFUSION U-NET starts with noisy static and slowly cleans it up, guided by that meaning-map, in a small compressed space.
3) A VAE DECODER takes that small clean result and blows it up into a full, detailed picture.
All three together = one smooth text-to-image pipeline.

cards.title: A Restaurant Kitchen
cards.body: Think of a restaurant: the WAITER (text encoder) listens to your order and writes it down clearly. The CHEF (U-Net) cooks the dish step by step in the kitchen, using that order as a guide, working with small prepped ingredients. The PLATER (VAE decoder) then arranges everything beautifully on a big plate for you to see. Three jobs, one meal!

cards.title: Follow the Prompt
cards.body: You type: 'a dragon reading a book.' Step 1: the text encoder turns these words into number-based meaning. Step 2: the U-Net starts with random noise in a tiny latent grid and removes noise bit by bit, always checking the meaning from step 1. Step 3: the VAE decoder expands that tiny clean latent grid into a big, sharp, full-color image of a dragon reading a book!

cards.title: Myth: One Big Brain Does It All
cards.body: Myth: Stable Diffusion is a single giant brain that just 'knows' how to paint. Truth: It's actually THREE separate specialized parts (text encoder, U-Net, VAE decoder) passing work to each other in a pipeline. No single part could make the final image alone!

cards.title: Old Way vs Full Pipeline
cards.body: Comparing a plain diffusion model working directly on pixels versus the complete Stable Diffusion pipeline working smartly in latent space.

left.text: Cleans noise directly on the full-size image — slow and heavy, like scrubbing every tile in a giant room one by one.

right.text: Text encoder + U-Net clean noise in a tiny latent space, then VAE decoder expands it — fast, guided, and detailed, like planning small then building big.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct order of the three main parts in the Stable Diffusion pipeline?
exercises.explain: First the text encoder reads the prompt, then the U-Net cleans noise in latent space guided by that meaning, and finally the VAE decoder expands it into the final image.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The U-Net in Stable Diffusion works directly on the full-size image, not on a compressed latent version.
exercises.explain: False! The U-Net works in a small, compressed latent space, which is why Stable Diffusion is faster than models that denoise full-size pixels directly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about the pipeline.
exercises.sentence: The ___ takes the small, clean latent result and expands it into a big, detailed final image.
exercises.explain: The VAE decoder is the last step — it blows up the tiny latent picture into the full-size image you finally see.

exercises.prompt: Which part of the pipeline is responsible for turning your typed words into a meaning-map the rest of the system can use?
exercises.explain: The text encoder is the very first stop — it reads and understands your prompt so the rest of the pipeline knows what to create.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Stable Diffusion just paints pixels directly from noise using one single network — no text understanding, no compression, just one step.' What's wrong with this claim?

options.text: Nothing — that's exactly how it works
It ignores that Stable Diffusion uses THREE parts: a text encoder to understand words, a U-Net to denoise in compressed latent space, and a VAE decoder to expand to the final image
It's wrong only because there's no text encoder involved at all
It's wrong only because the U-Net works on full-size pixels instead of latents

exercises.explain: The full pipeline needs all three specialized parts working together — text encoder for meaning, U-Net for guided denoising in tiny latent space, and VAE decoder for expanding to the final detailed image. No single part can do it all alone.
